OUTDOOR_BRIGHT_SPORTS.rationale: 1/2000s freezes peak action in full sunlight. Use 3D/Zone tracking AF to maintain focus on fast-moving subjects.
OUTDOOR_BRIGHT_SPORTS.proTip: Anticipate the decisive moment—start the burst 0.5s before the peak action, like a jump, collision, or goal.
OUTDOOR_BRIGHT_SPORTS.commonMistake: Firing a single shot at the wrong moment instead of using burst mode to guarantee capturing the peak action.

OUTDOOR_OVERCAST_SPORTS.rationale: Overcast reduces light; ISO raised to maintain 1/1000s. f/2.8 telephoto gives bokeh separation from busy backgrounds.
OUTDOOR_OVERCAST_SPORTS.proTip: Overcast actually improves sports—soft, even light means no harsh shadows across faces and no squinting.
OUTDOOR_OVERCAST_SPORTS.commonMistake: Using f/8 to get more depth—at sports distances, f/2.8 still keeps the whole player sharp enough.

INDOOR_ARENA_WELL_LIT.rationale: Indoor arenas are significantly dimmer than outdoors. Manual avoids flicker exposure inconsistency with artificial lighting.
INDOOR_ARENA_WELL_LIT.proTip: Select manual mode in arenas to avoid the camera hunting for exposure as players move across bright and dark zones.
INDOOR_ARENA_WELL_LIT.commonMistake: Auto exposure hunts between bright court and dark stands, causing inconsistent exposures between frames.

NIGHT_STADIUM.rationale: Stadium lighting is sufficient for 1/1000s at f/2.8 ISO 6400. Manual eliminates flicker banding from stadium lights.
NIGHT_STADIUM.proTip: Set shutter to a multiple of your stadium lighting frequency (1/100, 1/200, 1/500 for 50Hz) to avoid banding.
NIGHT_STADIUM.commonMistake: Using Auto ISO causes the camera to hunt constantly during night games with mixed lighting zones.

MOTORSPORTS_FREEZE.rationale: 1/2000s freezes spinning wheels and fast motion. f/5.6 maintains depth for entire car length.
MOTORSPORTS_FREEZE.proTip: Pre-focus on the spot you want to capture and shoot as the car enters zone. Manual pre-focus often beats tracking at extreme speed.
MOTORSPORTS_FREEZE.commonMistake: Continuous AF can struggle with extremely fast motorsports—pre-focus and use the zone for best results.

MOTORSPORTS_PANNING.rationale: Panning at 1/60s with a fast car creates a sharp car against a dramatically blurred background. ND filter prevents overexposure.
MOTORSPORTS_PANNING.proTip: The best panning images have a perfectly sharp car against streaked background—this requires practice. Aim for 1 in 20 keepers.
MOTORSPORTS_PANNING.commonMistake: Expecting good panning results on the first attempt—this requires significant practice to master.

SNOW_SPORTS.rationale: Snow is highly reflective—add +1.5 EV compensation or snow renders grey. Fast shutter freezes snow spray.
SNOW_SPORTS.proTip: Apply +1 to +1.5 EV exposure compensation when metering in snow to prevent grey snow and dark subjects.
SNOW_SPORTS.commonMistake: Not compensating for snow brightness creates underexposed skiers against grey-white backgrounds.

POORLY_LIT_GYM.rationale: Extremely dark gyms require maximum ISO. Modern sensors handle 12800 well. Manual mode prevents exposure hunting.
POORLY_LIT_GYM.proTip: Accept that gym shots will be noisy and focus on peak action—noise is better than missing the decisive moment.
POORLY_LIT_GYM.commonMistake: Staying at ISO 1600 for cleanliness results in shutter speeds too slow to freeze fast gym sport movements.

BLACK_AND_WHITE_SPORTS.rationale: B&W sports photography emphasizes emotion, effort, and form. Strong directional light creates dramatic shadows.
BLACK_AND_WHITE_SPORTS.proTip: Shoot RAW and use the red channel to darken skies and make skin tones pop in the monochrome conversion.
BLACK_AND_WHITE_SPORTS.commonMistake: Converting flat, even-lit sports photos results in grey, low-contrast images with no visual interest.

REFLECTIONS_SPORTS.rationale: Wet track/court surfaces create dramatic mirror effects. Fast shutter freezes both action and reflection.
REFLECTIONS_SPORTS.proTip: Rain during outdoor sports creates stunning puddle reflections—don't pack up when it rains!
REFLECTIONS_SPORTS.commonMistake: Only composing for the athlete and missing the reflection—step back to include both in the frame.

SWIMMING_POOL_AQUATICS.rationale: Indoor pool lighting is typically tungsten or fluorescent at EV 8-10. 1/1000s freezes water splashes and swimmer arms. f/2.8 is mandatory to maintain adequate exposure.
SWIMMING_POOL_AQUATICS.proTip: Shoot just after the turn — swimmers are fastest and create the most dynamic water spray. The peak dramatic moment is breaching the surface at the wall.
SWIMMING_POOL_AQUATICS.commonMistake: Underestimating competitive swimmer speed — 1/500s still shows motion blur at Olympic or competitive level. Use 1/1000s minimum for crisp freeze frames.

CYCLING_ROAD_PANNING.rationale: Panning with cyclists at 40-60km/h requires 1/60-1/125s to blur the background while keeping the rider sharp. f/8 provides depth margin if the pan timing isn't perfect.
CYCLING_ROAD_PANNING.proTip: Position yourself at exactly 90 degrees to the road for maximum panning blur effect. Follow the rider for at least 2 full seconds before pressing the shutter.
CYCLING_ROAD_PANNING.commonMistake: Panning from the shoulders — this creates unwanted vertical movement that blurs the rider vertically. Pan exclusively from the waist, keeping the camera perfectly level throughout.

BOXING_MMA_INDOOR.rationale: Combat sports arenas are typically EV 6-8. Punches reach 30-50km/h at impact and require 1/1000s minimum to freeze cleanly. f/2.8 is mandatory for adequate exposure.
BOXING_MMA_INDOOR.proTip: Focus on the gloves rather than the face for decisive impact moments. Anticipate the punch and begin the burst half a second before the glove connects.
BOXING_MMA_INDOOR.commonMistake: Shooting at 1/500s — arm speed in combat sports makes gloves completely blurred at that speed. 1/1000s is the absolute minimum for clean punch freeze frames.

EQUESTRIAN_JUMP.rationale: Horse and rider at the apex of a jump — the body is momentarily suspended. 1/2000s freezes hooves cleanly mid-air with no motion blur on the legs.
EQUESTRIAN_JUMP.proTip: Position at the far side of the fence, not the approach side — this gives you the profile shot with horse and rider in full suspended form. Start the burst three strides before the jump.
EQUESTRIAN_JUMP.commonMistake: Firing the shutter too late — the peak moment is when all four hooves are off the ground at the apex. Start the burst before the horse leaves the ground and let the buffer capture the peak.
